topic: Two coworkers discover they went to the same summer camp
raw_output:
[story 1]

The shared glance across the breakroom was a slow-motion detonation. Sarah’s jaw tightened so hard I could practically hear the muscle strain. "No way," she breathed, her voice dangerously low. I felt a heat rising in my chest, a prickly sensation that made my palms sweat. "You actually went to Camp Whispering Pines?" I managed, my tone clipped, every word measured and sharp. I leaned against the counter, crossing my arms tightly over my chest, refusing to look at her. The memory of that sweltering, mud-caked cabin suddenly felt like a personal affront. "I did," she confirmed, her eyes flashing with a kind of bewildered realization that only fueled my irritation. "How are you even saying that?" I scoffed, pushing off the counter with unnecessary force. "It’s just… impossible. We’ve worked together for five years, and this is how it comes up?" The sheer audacity of the coincidence made my throat feel tight.

[story 2]

I stared at the brochure she’d carelessly left on my desk—a faded, sun-bleached flyer for Camp Serenity. My knuckles were white where I gripped the edge of the laminate. When she finally looked up, her expression was a mixture of shock and dawning horror. "Wait a second," she murmured, her voice tight. "Did you go to Camp Serenity last summer?" The air in the office seemed to thicken, pressing down on my shoulders. A tight knot formed beneath my sternum, a restless energy vibrating just beneath my skin. I pushed my chair back, the scrape of metal on the floor sounding deafening in the quiet space. "Of course, I did," I snapped, my voice pitched higher than intended. I paced three steps toward the window, my breathing shallow and rapid. "It’s ridiculous. We’re in the same building, sharing spreadsheets, and this is the universe deciding to play cruel little jokes?" I stopped pacing abruptly, turning to face her, my gaze hard and unwavering.

[story 3]

When she mentioned the canoe trip, a cold, hard pressure settled behind my eyes. I felt a sudden, intense need to be somewhere else, anywhere but here, where this absurd connection was being unearthed. "You were at the lake retreat?" I asked, my voice dangerously level, devoid of any warmth. My hands clenched into fists deep in my pockets, the tension radiating outward. She looked genuinely startled, her initial surprise quickly morphing into something akin to alarm. "Yes," she admitted, avoiding my eyes for a moment before meeting them. "Camp Willow Creek. It was… intense." A muscle twitched near my temple. I let out a short, humorless exhale. "Intense. Right. Because finding out that we both spent three weeks sharing lukewarm s’mores under a canopy of stars is exactly what I needed today," I stated, my tone dripping with sarcasm that was almost too controlled. I turned sharply toward the door, needing space, needing to physically escape the suffocating reality of the shared history.
